{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks where supporting cusps occlude. From the provided documents, the most relevant information comes from Document [8] (Title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy), which states: 'The maxillary palatal cusps and the mandibular facial cusps comprise the centric cusps involved in centric occlusion (discussed below). In contrast, the maxillary buccal cusp and the mandibular lingual cusps guide the mandible during the lateral excursion.' This indicates that the supporting cusps (specifically the palatal cusp of the maxilla and the facial cusp of the mandible) are involved in centric occlusion and occlude in the central fossa of the opposing tooth. Document [13] (Title: Posterior Crossbite -- Introduction) further supports this by stating: 'In the transverse dimension, normal occlusion is when the palatine cusps of the upper molars and premolars occlude in the fossa of the lower molars and premolars.' This directly confirms that the palatine (supporting) cusps occlude in the central fossa. Marginal ridges and embrasures are not described as the site of supporting cusp occlusion. Therefore, the correct answer is A: Central fossa.", "answer_choice": "A"}
